title: Sticky Default
description: This function adds an item to a cart. Oddly, when called multiple times without passing a cart, items from previous calls reappear.
expectedBehavior: When no cart is provided, it should create a fresh empty cart and add the item.
hints: In Python, default arguments are evaluated only once when the function is defined.
Because a list is mutable, the same list object is reused every time the function is called without a cart argument.
Set the default argument to None, and inside the function, create a new list if it is None.

title: Lazy Lambdas
description: This function creates a list of 3 multiplier functions. However, all three functions end up multiplying by 2 instead of 0, 1, and 2.
expectedBehavior: The returned functions should multiply their input by 0, 1, and 2 respectively.
hints: Lambdas in Python use late binding; they look up the value of variables at the time they are called, not when they are created.
By the time the lambdas are called, the loop has finished and 'i' is equal to 2.
Force early binding by using a default argument in the lambda, like 'lambda x, i=i: x * i'.

title: Off by One
description: This code iterates over a list to create a formatted string. It crashes with an IndexError on the last element.
expectedBehavior: It should successfully combine all items in the array.
hints: Python lists are zero-indexed, meaning the first element is at index 0.
The highest valid index in a list is len(arr) - 1.
Adjust the range to start at 0 and end at len(arr).

title: Lost the Sort
description: This function attempts to sort an array of numbers and return the sorted array. Instead, it returns None.
expectedBehavior: Return the sorted version of the array.
hints: In Python, list methods that modify the list in-place (like .sort() or .append()) generally return None.
To return a sorted copy of a list, there is a built-in function you can use instead of the method.
Use the built-in sorted(arr) function.

title: Double Append Illusion
description: This function takes a list, appends 'x' for every existing item in the list, but it gets stuck in an infinite loop allocating memory until it crashes.
expectedBehavior: If the list has 3 items, it should iterate exactly 3 times and append 'x' 3 times.
hints: You are iterating over a list while simultaneously adding new items to the end of it.
Because the list keeps growing, the loop never reaches the 'end'.
Try iterating over a fixed copy of the list, or using the length of the list in a range loop.

title: Immutable Letters
description: This function attempts to capitalize the first letter of a word by directly assigning a capital letter to index 0, causing a TypeError.
expectedBehavior: Return a new string with the first letter capitalized.
hints: In Python, strings are immutable. You cannot change a specific character by index.
You must create a completely new string.
Try concatenating the upper-cased first letter with the rest of the string using slicing.

title: Forgotten Replace
description: This function is supposed to replace all spaces with underscores, but the original string is returned unchanged.
expectedBehavior: Return the modified string with underscores.
hints: String methods like replace() do not modify the original string in place.
They return a brand new string.
You need to assign the result of the replace method back to the variable, or return it directly.

title: Identity Crisis
description: This function checks if a string is equal to 'admin'. It works for simple variables but fails mysteriously when the string is constructed dynamically.
expectedBehavior: Return True if the value of the string is 'admin'.
hints: The 'is' operator checks for object identity (if they occupy the same memory location).
Strings constructed at runtime might have the same value but live in different memory locations.
Use the '==' operator to check for value equality.

title: Missing Key Crash
description: This function checks the 'status' of a user configuration. If 'status' isn't in the dictionary, it crashes with a KeyError.
expectedBehavior: Return False gracefully if the 'status' key is missing.
hints: Accessing a dictionary key directly using bracket notation raises a KeyError if the key doesn't exist.
You can check if a key is in the dictionary first using the 'in' keyword.
Alternatively, use the dictionary .get() method, which returns None if the key is missing.

title: Changing the Tracks
description: This function removes keys with empty string values from a dictionary. It crashes with a RuntimeError: dictionary changed size during iteration.
expectedBehavior: Successfully remove empty strings without crashing.
hints: Python strictly forbids changing the size of a dictionary while you are looping through it.
You need to separate the task of finding the keys from deleting them.
Try iterating over a list of the keys instead, or build a brand new dictionary.
